summary: Sitting in the Azarah is prohibited except for Davidic kings. The Great Assembly restored the full divine epithets to prayer; aggadah of capturing the yetzer hara for idolatry. HP reads standing; skipping rules in Torah reading.

display.whats: Sitting in the Temple Azarah is prohibited - only Davidic kings were permitted to sit there, derived from King David sitting before God (2 Sam. 7:18). The HP stood throughout the Torah reading ceremony.
display.hint: What is the source that only Davidic kings may sit in the Azarah?
display.title: Standing to Serve: Why Only Davidic Kings May Sit in the Azarah

learning.ahaMoment: The entire Temple service is characterized by standing - the kohanim 'stand to serve.' The exception for Davidic kings is not about comfort but about the king's unique status as God's anointed: the king's sitting before God is itself a form of reverence.
learning.memoryAnchor: Only Davidic kings sit in the Azarah. Everyone else stands.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why must the HP stand throughout the Yom Kippur service, and who is the only person permitted to sit in the Temple Azarah?
learning.coreTension: The HP is the highest religious official. If anyone deserves the honor of sitting, it should be him. Yet even he must stand. What makes a Davidic king's sitting different from the HP's?
learning.coreMove: All Temple service is performed standing - the kohanim 'stand to serve' (Deut. 18). The HP is no exception. Only Davidic kings may sit in the Azarah, derived from David sitting before God (2 Sam. 7:18). This royal exception reflects kingship, not religious rank.

takeaway.text: Standing is the posture of all Temple service. Only Davidic kings receive the exceptional permission to sit in the Azarah - religious rank (HP) does not grant this privilege, only royal lineage does.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume the HP's supreme religious status would entitle him to sit. The distinction is between religious and royal authority: the sitting privilege belongs to kingship, not priesthood.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: No sitting in the Azarah - only Davidic kings permitted
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Sitting in the Azarah is prohibited - the verse says 'to stand to serve.' Only Davidic kings are permitted to sit there, from David who 'came and sat before God' (2 Sam. 7:18).

halachic: prohibition of sitting in the Azarah
theological: standing as the posture of service
the king's unique status before God
narrative: David sitting before God after Nathan's prophecy

misconceptions.misconception: Any dignitary of sufficient rank may sit in the Azarah
misconceptions.correction: Only Davidic kings are permitted to sit in the Azarah - even the HP must stand throughout the service.

visualizableElements.description: Everyone in the Azarah standing in service vs Davidic king seated before God

quizSeeds.question: Who is permitted to sit in the Temple Azarah?
quizSeeds.answer: Only Davidic kings - all others, including the HP, must stand. This is derived from David sitting before God (2 Sam. 7:18) and from the verse 'to stand to serve' (Deut. 18).

sugyot.finalRuling: No sitting in the Azarah - only Davidic kings may sit there. The HP stood throughout the Torah reading.

display.whats: The Great Assembly restored the full divine epithets 'great, mighty, and awesome' to prayer - epithets that Jeremiah and Daniel had omitted during the Temple's destruction. Aggadah: the evil inclination (yetzer hara) for idolatry was captured and imprisoned by the Anshei Knesset HaGedolah.
display.hint: How could prophets like Jeremiah and Daniel - who spoke the truth - omit attributes of God that Moses had written in the Torah?
display.title: When Prophets Omitted God's Praises: The Great Assembly's Response

learning.ahaMoment: The prophets were not wrong - they legitimately could not call God 'mighty' while they saw His children enslaved. The Great Assembly reconciled this by redefining what 'mighty' and 'awesome' mean: constraint and patience ARE power, and Israel's survival IS God's awesomeness.
learning.memoryAnchor: Jeremiah omits 'awesome,' Daniel omits 'mighty' - Great Assembly restores both by redefining them as patience and survival.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why did Jeremiah and Daniel each omit one of God's traditional attributes from prayer, and how did the Great Assembly restore them?
learning.coreTension: Standard Jewish liturgy praises God as 'the great, mighty, and awesome.' Two prophets omitted attributes - Jeremiah omitted 'awesome,' Daniel omitted 'mighty.' Were they wrong? Can liturgy vary based on historical experience?
learning.coreMove: The prophets were not wrong - they acted with integrity. Jeremiah could not call God 'awesome' while watching the Temple desecrated; Daniel could not call God 'mighty' while Israel was enslaved. The Great Assembly restored both by reinterpreting: God's might is demonstrated in sustaining patience; God's awesomeness is that Israel survived at all among hostile nations.

takeaway.text: The Great Assembly did not criticize Jeremiah and Daniel for their omissions but reinterpreted the attributes to show they remained true even in catastrophe. Authentic prayer must be honest about experience.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume the prophets made errors corrected by the Great Assembly. The Gemara's logic is that both the omissions AND the restoration were correct - the prophets were honest, and the Great Assembly's reinterpretation validated the attributes without denying that honesty.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Great Assembly restored the three epithets to prayer
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Anshei Knesset HaGedolah restored 'great, mighty, and awesome' to prayer, which the prophets had omitted.

argumentFlow.label: Reason: Jeremiah omitted 'awesome' - pagans desecrated the Temple
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Jeremiah said: pagans are shouting in His Temple - where is His awesomeness? So he omitted 'awesome.'

argumentFlow.label: Reason: Daniel omitted 'mighty' - pagans enslaved Israel
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Daniel said: pagans are enslaving His children - where is His might? So he omitted 'mighty.'

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Great Assembly redefined might as patience
argumentFlow.speaker: Anshei Knesset HaGedolah
argumentFlow.text: This is His might - that He suppresses His inclination, extends patience for all the decrees against His children.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Great Assembly redefined awesomeness as Israel's survival
argumentFlow.speaker: Anshei Knesset HaGedolah
argumentFlow.text: These are His awesomeness - were it not for His fear, all nations would have gathered to destroy Israel but they survived.

argumentFlow.label: Narrative: Evil inclination for idolatry captured and imprisoned
argumentFlow.speaker: Aggadah
argumentFlow.text: The Anshei Knesset HaGedolah captured the yetzer hara for idolatry, imprisoned it in a lead pot - but the world could not function without the reproductive drive, so they had to release it partially.

argumentFlow.label: Narrative: When imprisoned, chickens stopped laying eggs
argumentFlow.speaker: Aggadah
argumentFlow.text: When the inclination was imprisoned, even a fresh egg could not be found - showing that reproductive drive sustains all life.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Released to half-power - desire for one's spouse only
argumentFlow.speaker: Aggadah
argumentFlow.text: They released it at half strength - it would still drive a man toward his wife but not toward another's wife.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: HP read the Torah while standing
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The HP read the Torah standing - consistent with the prohibition of sitting in the Azarah.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Skipping within the same topic is permitted
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: One may skip from Lev. 16 to Lev. 23 since both deal with Yom Kippur - they are in the same topic. The skip must be accomplished before the translator finishes the previous verse.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: No skipping between different topics even briefly
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Between different topics one may not skip at all - because Torah words (warnings, punishments, commandments) require focused listening, and jumping topics breaks concentration.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Burning of bull and goat happens while HP wears white
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The burning of the bull and goat happens while the HP still wears white linen vestments - he does not see the burning, implying it occurs before his third immersion.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Eimurim burned after third immersion with rams
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The eimurim (sacrificial parts) of the sin offering are burned at the third immersion together with the HP's ram and the people's ram.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Service sequence derived from scripture order
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The sequence of the Yom Kippur service follows the order in Leviticus - the Azazel dispatch precedes the burning of fats as written in the text.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: 'The dispatcher' refers to an earlier action despite later textual position
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The verse about 'the one who dispatches the goat' is positioned after the eimurim text but refers to an earlier act - like a speaker who interrupts and then resumes the original topic.

halachic: skipping in Torah reading - same topic vs different topic

requiresUnderstanding: Anshei Knesset HaGedolah (Men of the Great Assembly)

misconceptions.misconception: Jeremiah and Daniel were wrong to omit divine attributes from prayer
misconceptions.correction: The prophets acted truthfully - they could not praise God as 'mighty' or 'awesome' given what they witnessed. The Great Assembly reconciled this by reinterpreting what these attributes mean in context.

visualizableElements.description: Three prophets and their omissions: Moses (all three), Jeremiah (omits awesome), Daniel (omits mighty), Great Assembly (restores all three)

quizSeeds.question: Why did Jeremiah omit the word 'awesome' from his prayer?
quizSeeds.answer: Because pagans were desecrating the Temple - he could not call God 'awesome' while seeing His House defiled. The Great Assembly later restored it, explaining that Israel's survival among hostile nations IS God's awesomeness.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Great Assembly restored all three epithets (great, mighty, awesome) to prayer, reinterpreting them through the lens of God's patience and Israel's survival. The HP reads standing; skipping in Torah reading is permitted only within the same topic.

display.whats: The Leviticus 23 passage was read by heart (not from the scroll) because it was close to the Leviticus 16 portion - rolling the scroll there would not cause a delay for the congregation. The eight blessings that followed are listed.
display.hint: Why was it necessary to explain that no Torah scroll was brought out for the Leviticus 23 reading?
display.title: Lev. 16 and Lev. 23: Scroll and Memory in the HP's Torah Reading

learning.ahaMoment: The 'by heart' reading was actually a protective measure for the Torah scroll's honor - if people saw the HP reading by heart, they might think that passage was missing from the scroll. So he declared afterward: 'More is written here than what I read.'
learning.memoryAnchor: Lev. 16 from scroll, Lev. 23 by heart - then declare 'more is written here' to protect the scroll's honor.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why did the HP read Leviticus 16 from the scroll but recite Leviticus 23 by heart, and what was the declaration he made afterward?
learning.coreTension: Normally one reads Torah from the scroll. Reading by heart seems less formal. Why is reading by heart acceptable here, and what is the significance of the subsequent declaration?
learning.coreMove: Leviticus 23 is adjacent to Leviticus 16 in the scroll. Rolling to it while the congregation watches would be time-consuming and disrespectful. Reading by heart avoided this. Afterward, the HP declared 'more is written here than what I read' to protect the scroll's honor - signaling that the content exists in writing even though he read it from memory.

takeaway.text: Reading a Torah portion by heart is permitted when rolling the scroll would cause congregational embarrassment - and the follow-up declaration protects the Torah's honor by acknowledging the written source.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume the HP's by-heart reading was a leniency or concession. The Gemara explains it was actually the more respectful option - protecting both the congregation's time and the Torah scroll's dignity.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Leviticus 23 read by heart
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The HP read the Leviticus 23 (Emor) passage by heart, not from the scroll - because the portions are adjacent and rolling would not cause a congregational delay.

argumentFlow.label: Reason: Protect Torah scroll's honor
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If the congregation sees him read a third passage by heart, they might think that passage is missing from the scroll - a denigration of the Torah. Therefore he declared: 'more than I read is written here.'

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Eight blessings recited after readings
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: After the readings, the HP recited eight blessings: on Torah, service, thanksgiving, forgiveness, Temple, priests, Israel, and Jerusalem.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: The blessings recited in their standard formulation
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The blessings were recited as they appear in the Amidah - 'may You be pleased with Your people Israel' etc.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: 'Prayer for the rest' - for Israel's general needs
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: An additional prayer for general needs: supplication and petition before God for His people Israel who need salvation.

halachic: by-heart Torah reading on Yom Kippur
eight blessings of HP's Yom Kippur reading
theological: Torah scroll's sanctity and public perception
narrative: HP declaring 'more is written here' after reading by heart

misconceptions.misconception: Reading Torah by heart is always invalid
misconceptions.correction: While normally one reads from the scroll, the HP was permitted to read Leviticus 23 by heart since it was adjacent to the Lev. 16 portion and rolling there would not embarrass the congregation.

visualizableElements.description: HP reads Lev. 16 from scroll, then Lev. 23 by heart, holds rolled scroll to chest declaring 'more is written here,' then recites eight blessings

quizSeeds.question: Why did the HP recite Leviticus 23 by heart on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: The portion was adjacent to Leviticus 16 in the scroll. Rolling to it would cause congregational waiting, and reading it by heart was permitted. He then declared 'more is written here than what I read' to protect the scroll's honor.

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP read Lev. 16 from the scroll and Lev. 23 by heart, declared 'more is written here,' then recited eight blessings.

rashiTranslations.he: אין ישיבה בעזרה. דכתיב לעמוד לשרת (דברים יח) העומדים שם לפני ה' (שם) ולא מצינו בה ישיבה: אלא למלכי בית דוד שנאמר
rashiTranslations.en: There is no sitting in the Azarah - as it is written 'to stand to serve' (Deut. 18), 'those who stand there before Hashem' - and we find no sitting there.

rashiTranslations.he: ויבא המלך דוד וישב וגו'. כשאמר לו נתן הנביא אתה לא תבנה הבית כי ימלאו ימיך וגו': ויברך עזרא וגו'. בההוא ענינא דויקרא לפני
rashiTranslations.en: And King David came and sat - when Nathan the prophet told him: you shall not build the House, for your days will be fulfilled etc. And Ezra blessed.

rashiTranslations.he: הרחוב כתיב ברוך ה' אלהים וגו' כך תיקנו באותו היום לומר במקדש בסוף כל ברכה וברכה כדאמרינן במסכת תענית (דף יג.): בגבולין.
rashiTranslations.en: In the public square it is written: 'Blessed is Hashem God' etc. Thus they instituted on that day to say in the Temple at the end of each blessing as stated in the Gemara.

rashiTranslations.he: כל חוץ לעזרה קרי גבולין: בייא בייא. לשון זעקה וקובלנא: האי דאחרביה לבית המקדש. מבקשין רחמים שימסר בידם יצר הרע של
rashiTranslations.en: Everything outside the Azarah is called 'provincial.' Biya biya - an expression of outcry and complaint. The one who destroyed the Temple - they prayed for mercy.
Idolatry.
To accept the reward - let us bend it and receive the reward.
A note - a written message.

rashiTranslations.he: עמכם: אמר להו נביא. זכריה בן
rashiTranslations.en: True - meaning I agree with you.
The prophet told them - Zechariah son of Iddo.
A hair - a strand of hair. In a lead cauldron.

rashiTranslations.he: הרשעה. וישלך אותה אל תוך האיפה
rashiTranslations.en: In a lead cauldron. 'This is wickedness' - and he threw her into the ephah and put the lead stone over its mouth.
He absorbed the sound - the sound entered the lead and could not escape.
The world would be destroyed - there would be no procreation.
An egg laid that same day - because the heating process stopped in the hens' bodies, even those eggs already conceived could not be cooked.

rashiTranslations.he: עלמא. יכלה העולם שלא תהא פריה
rashiTranslations.en: To half - so that the inclination would control a man regarding his wife but not regarding another's wife.
They applied kohl to its eyes - they blinded it with kohl.

rashiTranslations.he: הנולדה בו ביום לפי שפסק החימום
rashiTranslations.en: The great, mighty, and awesome God - who shows no favoritism.
Pagans were desecrating His Temple - he saw Nebuchadnezzar's generals entering the Sanctuary and doing as they pleased.
He did not say 'awesome' - in the passage of Hanamel (Jer. 32) it says 'the great mighty God, Hashem of hosts is His name.'
Daniel did not say 'mighty' - in his prayer in his book (Dan. 9).
Pagans were enslaving his children - seventy years.

rashiTranslations.he: את עיניו ע"י כחול: האל הגדול
rashiTranslations.en: This is His strength - that He suppresses His inclination all those years while they are enslaved.

rashiTranslations.he: הגבור והנורא. אשר לא ישא פנים:
rashiTranslations.en: And extends His patience for all the decrees made against His children.
These are His awesomeness - were it not for His fear of Him etc. From the day of the destruction you learn His awesomeness.
All the nations gathered to destroy them yet they survived.

rashiTranslations.he: ומריעים בו ככל רצונם: לא אמר
rashiTranslations.en: And the Rabbis - Jeremiah and Daniel.
He is true - aligned with truth and hates falsehood.

rashiTranslations.he: עושה חסד לאלפים ומשלם עון אבות
rashiTranslations.en: Here when the translator can pause - this refers to the teaching that one may not skip in a distant passage.

rashiTranslations.he: אל חיק בניהם אחריהם האל הגדול
rashiTranslations.en: For before the Torah reader rolls the scroll to the place of the skip, the translator who translates each verse will pause.
And the congregation will sit waiting - this is not honoring the congregation. But 'only on the tenth' is near to 'Acharei Mot' and he can roll the scroll.

rashiTranslations.he: דניאל לא אמר גבור. בתפלתו בספרו
rashiTranslations.en: Before the translator pauses.
In one topic - such as here where both portions deal with Yom Kippur - one may skip, and as long as the translator does not pause.

rashiTranslations.he: בבניו. זה שבעים שנה: זו היא
rashiTranslations.en: But the reason one may not skip between two different topics even when the translator could pause is because Torah words.

rashiTranslations.he: גבורתו. שכובש יצרו כל השנים הללו
rashiTranslations.en: Are warnings, punishments, and commandments - they must enter the listeners' hearts.

rashiTranslations.he: שנשתעבדו בהן ומאריך אפו על כל
rashiTranslations.en: And when a person moves from one topic to another, it is not easy to focus.

rashiTranslations.he: הגזירות שגוזרים על בניו: ואלו הן
rashiTranslations.en: And it was taught - in a gentle tone.
He does not see the burning bull and goat - implying their burning happens while the HP still wears white vestments.

rashiTranslations.he: יום החורבן אתה למד נוראותיו שכל
rashiTranslations.en: And the eimurim of the sin offering - according to all opinions these are done afterward.
When he immerses a third time with the ram of the HP and the people's ram.
Say from this verse and onward - it was not written in order.

rashiTranslations.he: אמתי. מסכים על האמת ושונא את
rashiTranslations.en: The Mishnah's text was altered - should we say the burning of the bull and goat comes after the third immersion?
The verse says - and regarding the same language is written 'and the one who burns them.'
In the context of burning the bull and goat - and both are written after 'the fat of the sin offering.'
By necessity the Azazel dispatch precedes the burning of fats as written.
He sent the goat to the wilderness and then 'the fat of the sin offering' - by necessity 'the one who dispatches.'
Written after it - is like someone who interrupted the topic and returns to complete it.
And that dispatch I mentioned above - the one who dispatched it becomes tamei in garments.

rashiTranslations.he: אין זה כבוד צבור אבל אך בעשור
rashiTranslations.en: What about 'the one who dispatches' - even though it is written after the sin offering fats, it refers to what happened originally.

rashiTranslations.he: סמוכה היא לאחרי מות ויכול לגלול
rashiTranslations.en: So too 'the one who burns' - even though written after them, it refers to what happened originally.
On the contrary: 'the burner' is current - for the command for their burning is written after the sin offering fats.
Bring out and burn them. So too 'the dispatcher' is current - specifically written after the fats.
And the Azazel dispatch mentioned above - this is what it means: when the time for his dispatch comes.
'The dispatcher' refers to originally - like someone returning to a topic, as I explained.

rashiTranslations.he: דאפילו בכדי שלא יפסיק נמי לא
rashiTranslations.en: The Azazel dispatch is written in its own place - since it did not write again after the fats 'and he sent the goat.'

rashiTranslations.he: ידלג בשני ענינים לפי שדברי תורה
rashiTranslations.en: You say the Azazel dispatch was not written in its proper place - since he was not required to stand there until the time of atonement.
He found him in the market - he honors him in public saying: my master, the HP.
He sustains the living - He who gives life.
They took leave of each other - one took permission from the other to return home.
I shall walk - since it spoke of good lives, it cites this.
Where markets are - where a person can buy food; since David was constantly moving about, he prayed about this.
